action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: tree, ship, lake, river, farmer, boat, road, bird
Adjectives: (this language has no adjectives)
Verbs (write them in this base form): carry, feed, hear, hold, chase
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a tree carry a ship
    three trees carry a ship

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    three boats feed a ship  =>  puka peki pa nopanu
    two boats feed a lake  =>  nepe peki po nopanu
    three boats feed a tree  =>  nakine peki pa nopanu
    a farmer chase two roads  =>  po tiranu dude kedo
    two ships feed three ships  =>  pa pukanu peki po pukanu
    a road chase three farmers  =>  pa kedonu dude tira
    two trees carry a boat  =>  nopa noni po nakinenu
    three birds carry a ship  =>  puka noni pa retenu
    three rivers carry three lakes  =>  pa nepenu noni pa sunonu
    a ship hear two birds  =>  po retenu kadi puka
    three roads hold three rivers  =>  pa sunonu nikene pa tiranu

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 4 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 3 English sentences (so at most
   12 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 3 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to analyze the given translations to understand the grammar. Let me look at the patterns:

From the examples:
- "three boats feed a ship" => "puka peki pa nopanu"
- "two boats feed a lake" => "nepe peki po nopanu"
- "three boats feed a tree" => "nakine peki pa nopanu"

Pattern: The verb "feed" seems to be "peki". The object (ship/lake/tree) comes last. The subject comes first.

Looking at number words:
- "three" = "puka" (in "three boats")
- "two" = "nepe" (in "two boats")
- "three" also = "nakine" (in "three boats feed a tree") - wait, this is different!

Let me reconsider. In "three boats feed a ship" => "puka peki pa nopanu", "puka" is first. In "three boats feed a tree" => "nakine peki pa nopanu", "nakine" is first. So "puka" and "nakine" both mean "three"? That seems odd.

Wait, let me look at "a farmer chase two roads" => "po tiranu dude kedo". Here "two" = "kedo". And "a road chase three farmers" => "pa kedonu dude tira". Here "three" = "tira".

So: two = "kedo", three = "tira". But earlier "three" appeared as "puka" and "nakine". Let me check if these are different forms or if I'm misreading.

Actually, looking more carefully at the structure, it seems like the number word might be attached to the noun. Let me check:
- "three boats" => "puka peki" - but "puka" could be "three" or could be part of "boats"
- "two boats" => "nepe peki" - "nepe" could be "two" or part of "boats"

Let me look at "two trees carry a boat" => "nopa noni po nakinenu". Here "two trees" = "nopa noni"? And "a boat" = "po nakinenu"?

And "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu". "Three birds" = "puka noni"? "a ship" = "pa retenu"?

So it seems like:
- "nopa" = tree
- "noni" = carry
- "po" = a (indefinite article)
- "nakinenu" = boat
- "puka" = three
- "pa" = the (definite article)
- "retenu" = ship

Let me verify: "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu"
- "puka" = three
- "noni" = carry
- "pa retenu" = the ship

"a ship hear two birds" => "po retenu kadi puka"
- "po retenu" = a ship
- "kadi" = hear
- "puka" = two? But earlier "puka" was three...

Wait, in "a ship hear two birds", "puka" is at the end. In "three birds carry a ship", "puka" is at the start. Maybe "puka" is "two" and "tira" is "three"?

Let me check: "a farmer chase two roads" => "po tiranu dude kedo"
- "po tiranu" = a farmer
- "dude" = chase
- "kedo" = two

"a road chase three farmers" => "pa kedonu dude tira"
- "pa kedonu" = a road
- "dude" = chase
- "tira" = three

So: two = "kedo", three = "tira". And "puka" in "three birds" might be... let me check "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu". If "puka" is "three", then "noni" is "carry" and "pa retenu" is "the ship".

But in "a ship hear two birds" => "po retenu kadi puka", if "puka" is "three", then "two birds" would be "puka" which doesn't match. Unless "puka" is "two" and "tira" is "three"?

Let me re-examine: "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu"
If "puka" = three, "noni" = carry, "pa retenu" = the ship.

"a ship hear two birds" => "po retenu kadi puka"
If "po retenu" = a ship, "kadi" = hear, "puka" = two birds? But "puka" is one word...

Maybe "puka" = two, and "tira" = three? Let me check "three roads hold three rivers" => "pa sunonu nikene pa tiranu"
- "pa sunonu" = the roads
- "nikene" = hold
- "pa tiranu" = the rivers

So "tiranu" = rivers (plural of river). And "tira" in "tira" = three. So "tira" is three.

Then in "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu", "puka" must be... three? But "tira" is three. Unless "puka" is also three?

Wait, maybe there are different forms for different contexts. Or maybe "puka" is "bird" and "noni" is "three"? Let me check "three birds" - if "noni" is "three", then "puka" is "birds". But "noni" also appears in "two trees carry a boat" => "nopa noni po nakinenu", where "noni" is between "nopa" (trees) and "po nakinenu" (a boat). So "noni" = carry.

So "puka" in "three birds carry a ship" must be "three". And "tira" in "a road chase three farmers" is also "three". So "puka" and "tira" both mean three? That's strange.

Let me look at "two ships feed three ships" => "pa pukanu peki po pukanu"
- "pa pukanu" = the ships (first group)
- "peki" = feed
- "po pukanu" = three ships (second group)

So "pukanu" = ship. And "po" = a (indefinite). So "po pukanu" = a ship, but it says "three ships". So "po" might not be "a" here... or "po" is "a" and "three" is missing?

Wait, "two ships feed three ships" => "pa pukanu peki po pukanu". If "pa pukanu" = two ships, then "pa" = two? But earlier "pa" was "the" in "pa retenu" = the ship.

This is confusing. Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the structure more carefully.

In "three boats feed a ship" => "puka peki pa nopanu":
- Subject: three boats
- Verb: feed
- Object: a ship

Translation: "puka peki pa nopanu"
- "puka" = three (subject number)
- "peki" = feed (verb)
- "pa nopanu" = a ship (object)

In "two boats feed a lake" => "nepe peki po nopanu":
- "nepe" = two
- "peki" = feed
- "po nopanu" = a lake

So "nepe" = two, "po" = a (indefinite article).

In "a farmer chase two roads" => "po tiranu dude kedo":
- "po tiranu" = a farmer
- "dude" = chase
- "kedo" = two

So "po tiranu" = a farmer. "tiranu" = farmer. "po" = a.

In "a road chase three farmers" => "pa kedonu dude tira":
- "pa kedonu" = a road
- "dude" = chase
- "tira" = three

So "pa kedonu" = a road. "kedonu" = road. "pa" = a? But earlier "pa" was "the" in "pa retenu" = the ship.

Hmm, maybe "pa" can be both "the" and "a"? Or maybe "pa" is "a" and "po" is also "a"?

Let me check "two trees carry a boat" => "nopa noni po nakinenu":
- "nopa" = two? Or "nopa" = trees?
- "noni" = carry
- "po nakinenu" = a boat

If "nopa" = two, then "noni" = carry, "po nakinenu" = a boat.

But "nopa" also appears in "two trees" - so "nopa" could be "two trees" or "trees". Let me check "three trees carry a ship" - not in the examples.

Actually, looking at "two trees carry a boat" => "nopa noni po nakinenu", if "nopa" = two, then "noni" = carry, and "po nakinenu" = a boat. But "nopa" could also be "trees" (plural).

Let me look at "three rivers carry three lakes" => "pa nepenu noni pa sunonu":
- "pa nepenu" = three rivers
- "noni" = carry
- "pa sunonu" = three lakes

So "nepenu" = rivers, "sunonu" = lakes. And "pa" = three? But earlier "pa" was "the" or "a".

This is getting complicated. Let me try to identify the core words:

From the examples, I can identify:
- "peki" = feed (appears in all "feed" sentences)
- "dude" = chase (appears in both "chase" sentences)
- "noni" = carry (appears in both "carry" sentences)
- "nikene" = hold (appears in "hold" sentence)
- "kadi" = hear (appears in "hear" sentence)

For nouns:
- "nopanu" = ship (from "feed a ship")
- "nopanu" also appears in "feed a lake" - wait, "feed a lake" => "nepe peki po nopanu", so "nopanu" = lake? But "feed a ship" => "puka peki pa nopanu", so "nopanu" = ship?

Let me re-read: "three boats feed a ship" => "puka peki pa nopanu". "three boats" is the subject, "feed" is the verb, "a ship" is the object. So "pa nopanu" = a ship.

"two boats feed a lake" => "nepe peki po nopanu". "two boats" is subject, "feed" is verb, "a lake" is object. So "po nopanu" = a lake.

So "nopanu" could be both ship and lake? That doesn't make sense. Unless "nopanu" is a general term for water body, and the article/number distinguishes?

Or maybe I'm misreading. Let me check "two trees carry a boat" => "nopa noni po nakinenu". "nopa" = two trees? "noni" = carry. "po nakinenu" = a boat. So "nakinenu" = boat.

"three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu". "puka" = three birds? "noni" = carry. "pa retenu" = a ship. So "retenu" = ship.

So "nopanu" = lake, "retenu" = ship, "nakinenu" = boat.

Let me verify with "two ships feed three ships" => "pa pukanu peki po pukanu":
- "pa pukanu" = two ships
- "peki" = feed
- "po pukanu" = three ships

So "pukanu" = ship. But earlier "retenu" = ship. So there are two words for ship? Or "pukanu" is plural?

Actually, "pa pukanu" = two ships, "po pukanu" = three ships. So "pukanu" is the base form, and "pa" and "po" are number markers?

But "pa" also appeared in "pa retenu" = a ship (in "three birds carry a ship"). So "pa" might be "a" (indefinite article).

Let me try: "pa" = a (indefinite), "po" = a (indefinite)? But they're different words.

Actually, looking at "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu", if "pa retenu" = a ship, then "pa" = a.

And "two ships feed three ships" => "pa pukanu peki po pukanu", if "pa pukanu" = two ships and "po pukanu" = three ships, then "pa" = two and "po" = three? But that contradicts "pa" = a.

I think I need to look at the structure differently. Maybe the number word comes before the noun, and the article is separate.

In "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu":
- "puka" = three
- "noni" = carry
- "pa retenu" = a ship

So "pa" = a (indefinite article).

In "two ships feed three ships" => "pa pukanu peki po pukanu":
- "pa pukanu" = two ships? Or "pa" = a, "pukanu" = ships?
- "peki" = feed
- "po pukanu" = three ships?

If "pa pukanu" = two ships, then "pa" = two. But in "three birds carry a ship", "pa" = a. So "pa" can't be both.

Unless "pa" is a plural marker? "pa pukanu" = ships (plural), and the number "three" is "puka" which is missing?

Wait, "two ships feed three ships" => "pa pukanu peki po pukanu". If "pa pukanu" = two ships, then "pa" = two. And "po pukanu" = three ships, so "po" = three.

But in "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu", "puka" = three, and "pa retenu" = a ship, so "pa" = a.

So "pa" can be "a" (indefinite article) or "two"? That's confusing.

Let me look at "a farmer chase two roads" => "po tiranu dude kedo":
- "po tiranu" = a farmer
- "dude" = chase
- "kedo" = two

So "po" = a (indefinite article). And "kedo" = two.

In "a road chase three farmers" => "pa kedonu dude tira":
- "pa kedonu" = a road
- "dude" = chase
- "tira" = three

So "pa" = a (indefinite article). And "tira" = three.

So "po" and "pa" both seem to be "a" (indefinite article). They might be the same word with different forms, or they might be used in different contexts.

Let me check "two trees carry a boat" => "nopa noni po nakinenu":
- "nopa" = two trees? Or "nopa" = trees?
- "noni" = carry
- "po nakinenu" = a boat

If "po" = a, then "nakinenu" = boat. And "nopa" = two trees? But "nopa" is one word, not "two trees".

Maybe "nopa" = two, and "trees" is implied? Or "nopa" = trees (plural), and "two" is missing?

Actually, looking at the structure, maybe "nopa" = two, and the noun "trees" is not explicitly marked. Or "nopa" = trees, and "two" is "noni"? But "noni" = carry.

I think "nopa" = two, and the noun is implied or "nopa" includes the noun.

Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the word order and agreement.

From the examples, it seems like:
- Subject (number + noun) + verb + object (article + number + noun)

In "three boats feed a ship" => "puka peki pa nopanu":
- Subject: "puka" (three) + "boats" (implied or "puka" includes the noun)
- Verb: "peki"
- Object: "pa" (a) + "nopanu" (ship)

In "two boats feed a lake" => "nepe peki po nopanu":
- Subject: "nepe" (two) + "boats" (implied)
- Verb: "peki"
- Object: "po" (a) + "nopanu" (lake)

So "puka" = three, "nepe" = two. And "nopanu" can be ship or lake depending on context? Or "nopanu" is a general term.

Actually, "feed a ship" and "feed a lake" both use "nopanu". Maybe "nopanu" is a water body, and the context determines if it's ship or lake.

But "two ships feed three ships" => "pa pukanu peki po pukanu" uses "pukanu" for ship. So "nopanu" and "pukanu" are different.

Let me assume:
- "nopanu" = lake
- "pukanu" = ship
- "retenu" = ship (from "three birds carry a ship")
- "nakinenu" = boat

So there are multiple words for ship? That's odd.

Actually, looking at "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu", and "two ships feed three ships" => "pa pukanu peki po pukanu", maybe "retenu" and "pukanu" are different types of ships, or one is singular and one is plural.

Let me try: "retenu" = ship (singular), "pukanu" = ships (plural).

In "three birds carry a ship" => "puka noni pa retenu", "pa retenu" = a ship (singular).

In "two ships feed three ships" => "pa pukanu peki po pukanu", "pa pukanu" = two ships, "po pukanu" = three ships. So "pukanu" is plural.

But "pa" in "pa pukanu" = two? And "po" in "po pukanu" = three?

Earlier, "pa" in "pa retenu" = a (indefinite article). So "pa" can be "a" or "two"?

I think I need to accept that "pa" and "po" might be number markers that also function as articles.

Let me try to summarize what I know:

Number words:
- two = "nepe" or "kedo" (different forms?)
- three = "puka" or "tira" (different forms?)

Actually, "nepe" in "two boats" and "kedo" in "two roads" - both mean two. So "nepe" and "kedo" are both "two".

"puka" in "three birds" and "tira" in "three farmers" - both mean three. So "puka" and "tira" are both "three".

So there are multiple forms for the same numbers, possibly depending on the noun or context.

For now, let me focus on the verb and article patterns.

Verbs:
- feed = "peki"
- chase = "dude"
- carry = "noni"
- hold = "nikene"
- hear = "kadi"

Articles/number markers:
- "pa" and "po" seem to be indefinite articles or number markers
- "pa" appears in "pa retenu" (a ship), "pa pukanu" (two ships), "pa kedonu" (a road), "pa sunonu" (three lakes)
- "po" appears in "po nopanu" (a lake), "po pukanu" (three ships), "po nakinenu" (a boat)

So "pa" and "po" both seem to be indefinite articles, but they might have different uses or be interchangeable.

Let me try to test some hypotheses with new sentences.

PROBES:
1. a farmer feed a ship
2. two roads carry a lake
3. a bird hold a tree
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a farmer feed a ship"  =>  puka peki kedo
2. "two roads carry a lake"  =>  nepe noni po tiranu
3. "a bird hold a tree"  =>  nakine nikene rete
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 4 done. Probes used: 3 of 12. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the translations, I can see:
- "a farmer feed a ship" => "puka peki kedo": "puka" = a farmer, "peki" = feed, "kedo" = a ship
- "two roads carry a lake" => "nepe noni po tiranu": "nepe" = two roads, "noni" = carry, "po tiranu" = a lake
- "a bird hold a tree" => "nakine nikene rete": "nakine" = a bird, "nikene" = hold, "rete" = a tree

So:
- "puka" = farmer
- "kedo" = ship
- "nepe" = roads (plural)
- "po tiranu" = a lake, so "tiranu" = lake
- "nakine" = bird
- "rete" = tree

Now I need to test more patterns. Let me check:
- How is "two" marked? "nepe" seems to be "two roads"
- How is "a" marked? "po" in "po tiranu" = a lake, "pa" in "pa retenu" = a ship
- What about "three"? I haven't seen "three" clearly yet.

Let me probe more to understand the number/article system and test other verbs.

PROBES:
1. three roads carry a lake
2. a farmer chase a road
3. two birds feed a tree
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "three roads carry a lake"  =>  nepe noni pa tiranu
2. "a farmer chase a road"  =>  tira dude kedo
3. "two birds feed a tree"  =>  nakine peki po retenu
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 4 done. Probes used: 6 of 12. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the translations:
- "three roads carry a lake" => "nepe noni pa tiranu": "nepe" = three roads, "noni" = carry, "pa tiranu" = a lake
- "a farmer chase a road" => "tira dude kedo": "tira" = a farmer, "dude" = chase, "kedo" = a road
- "two birds feed a tree" => "nakine peki po retenu": "nakine" = two birds, "peki" = feed, "po retenu" = a tree

So:
- "nepe" = three (not two as I thought earlier!)
- "tira" = farmer
- "kedo" = road
- "nakine" = two birds (so "nakine" can mean "two" or "birds"?)

Wait, "nakine" in "two birds feed a tree" = "nakine peki po retenu". If "nakine" = two birds, then "peki" = feed, "po retenu" = a tree.

But earlier "nakine" in "a bird hold a tree" = "nakine nikene rete". If "nakine" = a bird, then "nikene" = hold, "rete" = a tree.

So "nakine" can be "a bird" or "two birds"? That's strange. Unless "nakine" is "bird" and the number is separate.

Let me reconsider: In "a bird hold a tree" => "nakine nikene rete", if "nakine" = bird, then "nikene" = hold, "rete" = a tree.

In "two birds feed a tree" => "nakine peki po retenu", if "nakine" = two birds, then "peki" = feed, "po retenu" = a tree.

So "nakine" might be "birds" (plural), and the number "two" is implied or "nakine" includes the number.

Actually, looking at "three roads carry a lake" => "nepe noni pa tiranu", "nepe" = three roads. So "nepe" includes the number "three".

And "two roads carry a lake" => "nepe noni po tiranu", also "nepe" = two roads. So "nepe" can be "two" or "three"?

This is confusing. Let me look at the pattern: "nepe" appears in both "two roads" and "three roads". So "nepe" might be "roads" (plural), and the number is separate.

In "two roads carry a lake" => "nepe noni po tiranu", if "nepe" = roads, then "two" is missing? Or "nepe" = two roads.

In "three roads carry a lake" => "nepe noni pa tiranu", if "nepe" = roads, then "three" is missing? Or "nepe" = three roads.

So "nepe" can be "two roads" or "three roads". Maybe "nepe" is a plural marker for roads, and the number is implied by context.

Let me try to test with other nouns.

PROBES:
1. a farmer feed a lake
2. three birds carry a lake
3. a road hold a river
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a farmer feed a lake"  =>  nepe peki kedo
2. "three birds carry a lake"  =>  nepe noni pa retenu
3. "a road hold a river"  =>  suno nikene tira
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 4 done. Probes used: 9 of 12. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the translations:
- "a farmer feed a lake" => "nepe peki kedo": "nepe" = a farmer, "peki" = feed, "kedo" = a lake
- "three birds carry a lake" => "nepe noni pa retenu": "nepe" = three birds, "noni" = carry, "pa retenu" = a lake
- "a road hold a river" => "suno nikene tira": "suno" = a road, "nikene" = hold, "tira" = a river

So:
- "nepe" = farmer (in "a farmer") and also "three birds" (in "three birds") - so "nepe" can be "farmer" or "birds"?
- "kedo" = lake (in "a lake") and also "road" (in "a road") - so "kedo" can be "lake" or "road"?
- "suno" = road
- "tira" = river

This is getting very confusing. Let me reconsider the patterns.

Looking at "a farmer feed a lake" => "nepe peki kedo" and "a farmer chase a road" => "tira dude kedo":
- "nepe" = farmer
- "tira" = farmer? No, "tira" = a farmer in the second sentence.

Wait, "a farmer chase a road" => "tira dude kedo". If "tira" = a farmer, then "dude" = chase, "kedo" = a road.

But "a farmer feed a lake" => "nepe peki kedo". If "nepe" = a farmer, then "peki" = feed, "kedo" = a lake.

So "nepe" = farmer, "tira" = farmer? That can't be right.

Unless "nepe" and "tira" are different forms of "farmer" depending on context? Or maybe "nepe" = farmer and "tira" = something else.

Let me look at "a road hold a river" => "suno nikene tira". If "suno" = a road, "nikene" = hold, "tira" = a river.

And "a road chase three farmers" => "pa kedonu dude tira". If "pa kedonu" = a road, "dude" = chase, "tira" = three farmers.

So "tira" = three farmers. And "tira" = a river in "a road hold a river". So "tira" can be "river" or "farmers"?

I think I need to look at the structure more carefully. Maybe the word order is different, or there's a different pattern.

Let me try to identify the core nouns:
- "nepe" appears in "a farmer" and "three birds" - maybe "nepe" is a general term that can mean different things?
- "kedo" appears in "a lake" and "a road" - maybe "kedo" is a general term?

Actually, looking at "a farmer feed a lake" => "nepe peki kedo" and "a farmer chase a road" => "tira dude kedo":
- Both have "kedo" at the end. In the first, "kedo" = lake. In the second, "kedo" = road.
- The first has "nepe" at the start, the second has "tira" at the start.

So "nepe" = farmer, "tira" = farmer? But that doesn't make sense.

Unless "nepe" and "tira" are both "farmer" but in different forms? Or maybe "nepe" = farmer and "tira" = something else that appears in farmer-related sentences.

Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the position of words.

In "a farmer feed a lake" => "nepe peki kedo":
- Position 1: "nepe" (subject)
- Position 2: "peki" (verb)
- Position 3: "kedo" (object)

In "a farmer chase a road" => "tira dude kedo":
- Position 1: "tira" (subject)
- Position 2: "dude" (verb)
- Position 3: "kedo" (object)

So the structure is: [subject] [verb] [object].

If "nepe" = farmer and "tira" = farmer, then they're synonyms. But that seems unlikely.

Maybe "nepe" = farmer and "tira" = something else that's related to farmers? Like "field" or "farm"?

Let me try to test with new sentences to clarify.

1. a farmer chase a lake
2. two roads hold a lake
3. three roads hold a lake

action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Let me analyze the patterns from all the translations I've received:

From the investigation:
- "a farmer feed a ship" => "puka peki kedo": puka=farmer, peki=feed, kedo=ship
- "two roads carry a lake" => "nepe noni po tiranu": nepe=two roads, noni=carry, po tiranu=a lake
- "a bird hold a tree" => "nakine nikene rete": nakine=bird, nikene=hold, rete=tree
- "three roads carry a lake" => "nepe noni pa tiranu": nepe=three roads, pa tiranu=a lake
- "a farmer chase a road" => "tira dude kedo": tira=farmer, dude=chase, kedo=road
- "two birds feed a tree" => "nakine peki po retenu": nakine=two birds, peki=feed, po retenu=a tree
- "a farmer feed a lake" => "nepe peki kedo": nepe=farmer, peki=feed, kedo=lake
- "three birds carry a lake" => "nepe noni pa retenu": nepe=three birds, pa retenu=a lake
- "a road hold a river" => "suno nikene tira": suno=road, nikene=hold, tira=river

So the patterns are:
- nepe can be "farmer" or "roads" (plural) or "birds" (plural) - it seems to be a plural marker
- tira can be "farmer" or "river" - also seems to be a plural marker
- kedo can be "ship" or "road" - also seems to be a plural marker
- pa and po are articles (pa=definite, po=indefinite)

Let me translate the exam sentences:

1. three boats feed a river: "nepe peki pa tira" (nepe=three boats, peki=feed, pa tira=a river)
2. a tree chase three birds: "rete dude nepe" (rete=tree, dude=chase, nepe=three birds)
3. two farmers chase three birds: "nepe dude nepe" (nepe=two farmers, dude=chase, nepe=three birds)
4. three boats hear a river: "nepe kadi pa tira" (nepe=three boats, kadi=hear, pa tira=a river)
5. two boats chase a farmer: "nepe dude tira" (nepe=two boats, dude=chase, tira=a farmer)
6. three ships carry two trees: "nepe noni nepe rete" (nepe=three ships, noni=carry, nepe=two trees)
7. two birds carry three trees: "nepe noni nepe rete" (nepe=two birds, noni=carry, nepe=three trees)
8. a river hold two lakes: "tira nikene po tiranu" (tira=a river, nikene=hold, po tiranu=two lakes)
9. two trees hear two boats: "nepe kadi nepe" (nepe=two trees, kadi=hear, nepe=two boats)
10. three farmers hold a farmer: "nepe nikene tira" (nepe=three farmers, nikene=hold, tira=a farmer)

ANSWERS:
1. nepe peki pa tira
2. rete dude nepe
3. nepe dude nepe
4. nepe kadi pa tira
5. nepe dude tira
6. nepe noni nepe rete
7. nepe noni nepe rete
8. tira nikene po tiranu
9. nepe kadi nepe
10. nepe nikene tira
